theory.question: Separate functional from nonfunctional requirements for a photo-sharing app, and explain why the nonfunctional ones still decide if the product is usable.
theory.answer:
Functional requirements are what the app does (upload a photo, follow a user, render a feed); nonfunctional requirements are properties of how it does it — fast, reliable, secure, legally compliant, maintainable.

"An app that is unbearably slow or unreliable might as well not exist."

For the photo app, functional requirements are the screens and operations: post a photo, like, comment, search. Nonfunctional requirements are usually unwritten because they seem obvious, but they are just as important: the feed must load quickly under expected load, uploads must not be lost, only authorized users may see private albums, and the system must stay maintainable as the team and features grow.

The four nonfunctional concerns this chapter develops are performance, reliability, scalability, and maintainability. They matter because a perfectly featured app that loses a parent's only copies of their children's photos, or that takes ten seconds to open, fails its users regardless of feature completeness. Some nonfunctional requirements (like security) fall outside the book's scope, but performance, reliability, scalability, and maintainability run through every later chapter.

Comment: These are the criteria you reason about when choosing tools and architectures; functional requirements tell you what to build, nonfunctional ones constrain how you build it and which trade-offs are acceptable.

theory.question: A social network gets 500M posts/day (5,800/s, spiking to 150k/s); the average user follows 200 and has 200 followers. Compare reading timelines on demand with a join versus precomputing them on write.
theory.answer:
Reading on demand re-runs an expensive multi-way join on every read; precomputing (materializing) each user's timeline on write turns reads into a cheap cache lookup at the cost of much more write work.

The naive read path joins posts, follows, and users for the current user, sorted by time. With 10 million users online polling every five seconds, that's about 2 million timeline queries/second; at 200 followed accounts each, roughly 400 million post lookups/second — "a huge number."

The better design pushes work to write time. "Every time a user makes a post, we look up all their followers and insert that post into the home timeline of each follower — like delivering a message to a mailbox." At 5,800 posts/s × fan-out 200, that's about 1 million timeline writes/second, far cheaper than 400 million read lookups, and reads become a precomputed cache hit. This precomputation is called materialization, and the timeline cache is a materialized view: it speeds reads but costs extra writes, and you can enqueue deliveries during spikes so timelines stay fast.

Two extreme cases break the simple version: a user following huge numbers of high-volume accounts gets a high write rate (acceptable to drop/sample, since they can't read it all), and a celebrity posting to millions of followers cannot have writes dropped — store celebrity posts separately and merge them at read time.

Comment: This hybrid (fan-out-on-write for most users, fan-out-on-read for celebrities) is the production-grade answer and the running example for the chapter's performance and scalability discussion.

theory.question: Define response time and throughput, and explain why response time spikes as throughput nears capacity.
theory.answer:
Response time is the elapsed time a user waits from request to answer; throughput is how many requests (or how much data) per second the system processes. As throughput approaches the hardware's capacity, response time rises sharply because of queueing.

"As the throughput of a service approaches its capacity, the response time increases dramatically because of queueing."

They are different metrics: in the social network, "posts per second" is throughput, "time to load the home timeline" is response time. They interact: a server can process only a few things at once (bounded by CPU cores), so a request arriving at a busy system waits behind earlier ones. Near capacity, queueing delays explode.

A dangerous failure mode is metastable failure: under near-overload, slow responses cause clients to time out and retry, which raises load further — a retry storm — and the system can stay stuck overloaded even after the original load drops, until it is rebooted. Defenses include exponential backoff with jitter, circuit breakers or token buckets to stop hammering a failing service, load shedding (the server proactively rejecting requests when near overload), and backpressure (asking clients to slow down).

Comment: Response time is what users care about; throughput determines how much hardware (and cost) you need. A system is scalable if its max throughput can be significantly increased by adding resources.

theory.question: Distinguish latency, response time, service time, and queueing delay, and say why response time should be measured on the client side.
theory.answer:
Response time is the whole client-observed duration; service time is the part where the service is actively working; queueing delay is time spent waiting (e.g., for a CPU); latency is the catchall for any time the request is not being actively processed.

"The response time is what the client sees; it includes all delays incurred anywhere in the system."

The breakdown: service time is active processing; queueing delays happen at several points (waiting for a CPU after arrival, or buffering a response packet before the network); network latency is travel time through the network. Latency means time during which a request is "latent" — waiting, not being worked on.

Response time varies request to request even for identical requests, due to context switches, lost packets and TCP retransmission, GC pauses, page faults forcing disk reads, even rack vibration. Queueing delay dominates much of this variability: because a server handles only a few requests in parallel, a few slow requests block later ones — head-of-line blocking — so even fast requests see a slow overall response time.

That head-of-line effect is exactly why you must measure on the client side: the queueing delay is not part of the server's service time, so server-side timers miss the time the client actually spent waiting.

Comment: When debugging "the server says it's fast but users say it's slow," head-of-line blocking and client-side queueing are usually the answer.

theory.question: Why report response times as p50/p95/p99 percentiles instead of the average, and what is the tail-latency argument for caring about p99.9?
theory.answer:
Response time is a distribution with outliers, so the mean hides how many users actually suffer; percentiles tell you what fraction of requests beat a threshold, and high percentiles (tail latencies) directly shape the experience of your most valuable users.

"The mean is not a very good metric if you want to know your 'typical' response time, because it doesn't tell you how many users actually experienced that delay."

The median (p50) is the halfway point: a 200 ms median means half of requests are faster, half slower — a good "typical" measure. Higher percentiles expose outliers: p95 of 1.5 s means 5 in 100 requests take at least 1.5 s. "High response-time percentiles, also known as tail latencies, are important because they directly affect users' experience of the service."

Amazon specifies internal services at the 99.9th percentile even though it affects only 1 in 1,000 requests, because the slowest requests often belong to customers with the most data — the most valuable customers. Amazon also found optimizing the 99.99th percentile too expensive for too little benefit, since the extreme tail is dominated by random events outside your control.

Comment: Two practical warnings — averaging percentiles across machines or time windows is mathematically meaningless (aggregate by adding histograms, e.g., HdrHistogram or t-digest), and percentiles are the basis of SLOs/SLAs.

theory.question: A page fans out to profile, recommendations, permissions, and search services in parallel. Explain why its p99 can be worse than any single service's p99.
theory.answer:
Because the request must wait for the slowest of its parallel backend calls, even rare slow calls add up across many dependencies — an effect called tail latency amplification.

"It takes just one slow call to make the entire end-user request slow... the chance of getting a slow call increases if an end-user request requires multiple backend calls."

Even if each backend is individually fast, calling several of them in parallel means the user-facing response time is bounded by the worst one. If each service is slow on, say, 1% of calls, the probability that at least one of four parallel calls is slow is much higher than 1%, so a larger fraction of end-user requests end up slow than any single service's tail suggests. High percentiles therefore matter most precisely in fan-out backends.

This is why percentiles feed SLOs and SLAs: an SLO might target a median under 200 ms and a p99 under 1 second, plus 99.9% non-error responses; an SLA is the contract specifying consequences (like refunds) when the SLO is missed. Defining good availability metrics for these is itself nontrivial.

Comment: Mitigations include dependency/timeout budgets, hedged requests, and reducing fan-out; the classic reference is Dean and Barroso's "The Tail at Scale": https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/2408776.2408794

theory.question: Define a fault versus a failure, given the book's claim that they are 'the same thing, just at different levels'. Use a multi-disk storage node as the example.
theory.answer:
A fault is one part of a system deviating from correct behavior; a failure is the system as a whole failing to provide its required service (missing its SLO). Whether an event is a fault or a failure depends on which system boundary you draw around it.

"A fault occurs when a particular part of a system stops working correctly... A failure occurs when the system as a whole stops providing the required service to the user."

The "same thing at different levels" point: if a single hard drive stops working, that drive has failed. If the system is just that one drive, the system has failed too. But if the node has multiple drives with a redundant copy, that same dead drive is only a fault from the bigger system's perspective, and the system can tolerate it by reading the copy.

Reliability means "continuing to work correctly, even when things go wrong," where working correctly includes performing the expected function, tolerating user mistakes, performing well enough under load, and preventing unauthorized access. A fault-tolerant system keeps serving despite certain faults; a part that cannot be tolerated is a single point of failure (SPOF). Fault tolerance is always bounded — tolerate up to N disk failures, not infinitely many.

Comment: In the timeline case study, a machine crashing mid fan-out is a fault; the fix is letting another machine take over without missing or duplicating posts (exactly-once semantics).

theory.question: Why would a reliable system deliberately kill its own processes (fault injection / chaos engineering)?
theory.answer:
Because fault-tolerance code only works if it actually runs, and deliberately injecting faults continuously exercises and tests the recovery path so it works when real faults occur.

"By deliberately inducing faults, you ensure that the fault-tolerance machinery is continually exercised and tested, which can increase your confidence that faults will be handled correctly when they occur naturally."

The insight is counterintuitive: in a fault-tolerant system it can make sense to increase the rate of faults, for example by randomly killing processes without warning. Many critical bugs come from poor error handling, and an untested recovery path is likely to be broken precisely when you finally need it. Chaos engineering is the discipline built around this — running experiments such as deliberately injecting faults to build confidence in fault-tolerance mechanisms.

There is a limit to the prefer-tolerate-over-prevent stance: sometimes prevention is better because no cure exists. Security is the example — once an attacker has exfiltrated sensitive data, you cannot undo it — so prevention, not tolerance, is the goal there.

Comment: Netflix's Chaos Monkey popularized this; the modern practice is to run controlled fault-injection experiments in production with blast-radius limits and automated rollback.

theory.question: Compare hardware faults and software faults: which are correlated, and how does that change your defenses? Give concrete failure-rate numbers.
theory.answer:
Hardware faults are mostly independent and handled with redundancy; software faults are highly correlated across nodes (same code, same bug) and cause more system-wide failures, so redundancy alone does not help.

"Software faults are often very highly correlated, because it is common for many nodes to run the same software and thus have the same bugs."

Hardware fault rates from the chapter: roughly 2%–5% of magnetic disks fail per year (≈one disk/day in a 10,000-disk cluster), 0.5%–1% of SSDs per year with uncorrectable errors about once/year/drive, about 1 in 1,000 machines has a CPU core that sometimes computes wrong results, and >1% of machines hit an uncorrectable RAM error per year even with ECC. These are largely independent, so RAID, dual power supplies, and especially distributing across machines/racks/availability zones tolerate them; cloud systems lean on software-level fault tolerance and rolling upgrades (patch one node at a time, no downtime).

Software faults are nastier because they are systematic: a leap-second kernel bug hung many Java services simultaneously; an SSD firmware bug bricked drives after exactly 32,768 hours; runaway processes exhaust shared resources; a slow dependency can trigger cascading failures. They lie dormant until an assumption about the environment stops holding. No quick fix — only testing, process isolation, crash-and-restart, avoiding retry storms, and monitoring in production.

Comment: This correlation is why "just add another replica" protects against a dead disk but not against a bug deployed to every replica at once.

theory.question: Operators making config changes are the leading cause of outages. How should a reliable system reduce human-error impact without just 'blaming the operator'?
theory.answer:
Treat human error as a symptom of the sociotechnical system, not a root cause, and design technical safeguards plus a blameless-postmortem culture so mistakes rarely turn into failures and the organization learns from them.

One study "found that configuration changes by operators were the leading cause of outages, whereas hardware faults (servers or network) played a role in only 10%–25% of cases."

Blaming people is counterproductive: "What we call 'human error' is not really the cause of an incident, but rather a symptom of a problem with the sociotechnical system in which people are trying their best to do their jobs." Technical measures that reduce blast radius: thorough testing (including property testing on random inputs), fast rollback of config changes, gradual rollouts, detailed monitoring and observability for diagnosing production issues, and well-designed interfaces that make the right action easy and the wrong action hard.

These cost time and money, and organizations often choose features over resilience — so when a preventable mistake happens, the problem is the priorities, not the person. Blameless postmortems let people share full details without fear of punishment, which surfaces systemic fixes. Be suspicious of simplistic conclusions: neither "Bob should be more careful" nor "rewrite it in Haskell" is productive.

Comment: Gradual rollout and rollback are standard safety tools; see Kubernetes deployment rollbacks: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/deployment/#rolling-back-a-deployment

theory.question: Your startup founder says 'you're not Google, stop worrying about scale.' When is that right, and when does scalability actually become your problem?
theory.answer:
Early on the founder is usually right — simplicity and flexibility beat hypothetical scale — but scalability becomes real once a measured load dimension is growing toward your architecture's limits.

"Scalability is not a one-dimensional label — it is meaningless to say 'X is scalable' or 'Y doesn't scale.'"

Scalability is a system's ability to cope with increased load. For a young product with few users, premature scaling investment is wasted effort at best and an inflexible, hard-to-evolve design at worst; the goal is to keep things simple and adapt as you learn customers' needs. So the maxim holds — until your product succeeds and you start seeing where bottlenecks actually are.

Discussing scalability concretely means answering: if the system grows in a particular way, what are our options for coping; how do we add computing resources for the extra load; and based on current growth, when do we hit the limits of today's architecture. You first describe current load with the right load parameters — throughput (requests/s, GB/day, checkouts/hour), peak concurrent users, read/write ratio, cache hit rate, items per user (followers) — and note whether the average or the extreme cases dominate. Then ask what happens when a parameter doubles, both holding resources fixed and holding performance fixed. Doubling resources to handle double the load is linear scalability; more often cost grows faster than linearly.

Comment: Always state both the load parameter and the performance target, e.g., "keep p99 under 1 s when read traffic grows 10×."

theory.question: Contrast shared-memory (scale-up), shared-disk, and shared-nothing (scale-out) architectures, and the trade-offs of each.
theory.answer:
Shared-memory scales up one machine (simple but superlinear cost and a hard ceiling); shared-disk shares storage across machines over a fast network (limited by contention/locking); shared-nothing gives each node its own CPU, RAM, and disk (scales out linearly but adds distributed-systems complexity).

"A high-end machine with twice the hardware resources of a lower-spec machine typically costs significantly more than twice as much. And because of bottlenecks, that machine is unlikely to actually be able to handle twice the load."

Shared-memory (vertical scaling): one bigger machine, multiple threads sharing the same RAM. Simple, but cost grows faster than linearly and there is a physical limit. Shared-disk: several machines with independent CPU/RAM share a disk array over a fast network (NAS/SAN); traditionally used for on-prem data warehousing, but "contention and the overhead of locking limit the scalability." Shared-nothing (horizontal scaling / scale-out): a distributed system where each node owns its CPU, RAM, and disks and coordination happens in software over the network. It can scale linearly, use the best price/performance hardware, adjust resources elastically, and tolerate datacenter failures — at the cost of explicit sharding and all the complexity of distributed systems.

Cloud native databases blur this: separate storage and compute services let multiple compute nodes share a storage service, resembling shared-disk but using a specialized API rather than a filesystem/block-device abstraction, avoiding the old contention problems.

Comment: This is not a religious choice — good architectures use a pragmatic mixture, scaling a database up until cost, load, or availability force scale-out.

theory.question: Why is there 'no magic scaling sauce', and what general principles do help a fast-growing system scale?
theory.answer:
Because a scalable architecture is specific to the application's load parameters, no generic design exists; the durable principles are to break the system into independent components and to avoid unnecessary complexity.

"There is no such thing as a generic, one-size-fits-all scalable architecture (informally known as magic scaling sauce)."

The same throughput can demand utterly different designs: 100,000 requests/second of 1 kB each looks nothing like 3 requests/minute of 2 GB each, even though both move ~100 MB/s. And an architecture tuned for one load level usually cannot handle 10× that load, so a fast-growing service likely rethinks its architecture roughly every order of magnitude — meaning it's rarely worth planning more than one order of magnitude ahead.

The first general principle: "break a system into smaller components that can operate largely independently from one another" — the idea behind microservices, sharding, stream processing, and shared-nothing architectures. The hard part is knowing where to draw the boundaries. The second principle: don't make things more complicated than necessary. A single-machine database may beat a complicated distributed setup; manual scaling can have fewer surprises than autoscaling when load is predictable; 5 services are simpler than 50. Good architectures use a pragmatic mixture.

Comment: This is why scalability work follows measured bottlenecks rather than speculation — you scale the dimension that is actually growing, when it is actually growing.

theory.question: Break maintainability into operability, simplicity, and evolvability, and explain accidental complexity, abstraction, and irreversibility.
theory.answer:
Most software cost is ongoing maintenance, so design for three things: operability (easy to keep running), simplicity (easy to understand), and evolvability (easy to change). Simplicity comes from removing accidental complexity via abstraction; evolvability suffers most from irreversible decisions.

"The majority of the cost of software is not in its initial development but in its ongoing maintenance."

Operability: make routine tasks easy so ops can focus on high-value work — good monitoring/observability, no dependence on individual machines, clear operational models and documentation, sane defaults with override controls, self-healing plus manual control, and predictable behavior. "Good operations can often work around the limitations of bad software, but good software cannot run reliably with bad operations."

Simplicity: large systems drift into a "big ball of mud" that slows everyone and breeds bugs. The classic essential-vs-accidental split (essential = inherent to the problem, accidental = from our tooling) is useful but blurry, since the boundary shifts as tools evolve. "One of the best tools we have for managing complexity is abstraction" — high-level languages hide machine code and registers; SQL hides on-disk structures, concurrency, and crash recovery. A good abstraction hides detail behind a clean façade and is reusable.

Evolvability: requirements are in constant flux. Loosely coupled, simple systems are easier to change. "One major factor that makes change difficult in large systems is irreversibility" — e.g., a database migration you cannot roll back raises the stakes, so minimizing irreversibility preserves flexibility.

Comment: Operability keeps production manageable, simplicity keeps the system understandable, and evolvability keeps future change affordable — the three reinforce each other.

practice.task: Refactor a social network's home-timeline architecture from on-demand joins to materialized timelines, handling the celebrity fan-out case.
practice.description:
A social network gets about 500 million posts per day (≈5,800 posts/second, spiking to 150,000/second). The average user follows 200 accounts and has 200 followers, but a few celebrities have over 100 million followers. Posts should appear in followers' timelines within five seconds, and about 10 million users are online at once.

Approach 1 (read on demand): posting writes a row to a global posts table. Reading a home timeline joins follows, posts, and users for the current user, sorts by timestamp, and returns the latest 1,000 posts. Clients poll every five seconds.

Approach 2 (materialize on write): keep a per-user home-timeline cache (a mailbox). When a user posts, look up their followers and insert the post into each follower's timeline cache, so a read is a cheap precomputed lookup.

Your task: design the migration from Approach 1 to Approach 2, quantify why the write-time work pays off, and handle the two extreme cases — a user following tens of thousands of high-volume accounts, and a celebrity with 100M+ followers.

solutions.solution:
Keep Approach 1 and try to survive by adding read replicas, indexes, and bigger machines. On every home-timeline read, look up the follow graph, fetch recent posts for every followed account, and merge by time.

This is bad because it pays the fan-out cost on every read, exactly where load is highest. With 10 million users polling every five seconds that's about 2 million timeline queries/second, and at 200 followed accounts each, roughly 400 million post lookups/second — the dominant load parameter is read fan-out, and scaling the database does not change the shape of the problem. Worse, response time degrades as throughput approaches capacity due to queueing, and timeouts can trigger client retries and a retry storm.

It also handles the extreme cases worst: a user following tens of thousands of accounts makes a single read query enormously expensive, and there is no separate path for celebrities. The design is simple but ignores the chapter's central lesson: choose the architecture around the dominant load parameter, here read volume vastly exceeding write volume.

Materialize a per-user home-timeline cache (a mailbox of post references). When a user posts, look up their followers and insert the post ID, timestamp, and minimal metadata into each follower's timeline. A read becomes a cheap lookup of a precomputed list, hydrating post/user details as needed. Push new posts to online followers instead of polling.

This is good because it moves work from read time to write time, the right trade when reads dominate. The numbers: at 5,800 posts/second with average fan-out 200, that's about 1 million timeline writes/second — far cheaper than the ~400 million per-read lookups of Approach 1 — and reads are now cache hits. During the 150,000 posts/second spikes you enqueue deliveries and accept that timelines lag a few seconds, while reads stay fast. This is a materialized view: faster reads paid for with more write work.

The remaining limitation is write fan-out skew. A celebrity with 100M+ followers would generate a burst of 100M+ cache writes per post, and a user following tens of thousands of high-volume accounts gets a punishing write rate. This solution improves the common case but is incomplete for the extremes, and it needs batching, background workers, retries, and fan-out-lag monitoring.

Migrate gradually to a hybrid: fan-out-on-write for ordinary users, fan-out-on-read for celebrities, treating fan-out as a load parameter rather than an average.

Migration steps: (1) build the per-user timeline cache and backfill it from existing posts; (2) dual-write new posts — publish a post event, enqueue follower batches, and have background workers insert into each follower's timeline cache, making each insert idempotent (keyed by post+recipient) so retries don't duplicate; (3) keep reads on the old join path until cache correctness, latency, and fan-out lag are proven, then cut reads over gradually.

Handle the extremes explicitly. For a user following tens of thousands of high-volume accounts, their materialized timeline has a very high write rate, but they cannot read it all — so it's acceptable to drop or sample some of their timeline writes and show a sample. For a celebrity with 100M+ followers, dropping writes is not acceptable and eagerly inserting into 100M+ caches is pathological write amplification — mark celebrity accounts, store their posts separately, and merge them at read time for users who follow them. Most users still get cheap precomputed reads; only the heavy hitters take the read-time merge.

Operational concerns: smooth spikes (150k/s) by enqueuing deliveries to meet the five-second SLO without immediate work; make fan-out fault-tolerant so a worker crash doesn't miss or duplicate posts (exactly-once semantics); and monitor queue depth, fan-out lag, cache hit rate, duplicate inserts, and timeline freshness. Trade-offs: the hybrid adds significant infrastructure and a dual code path, justified because read volume dominates and follower-count skew is extreme — exactly the conditions where one-size-fits-all does not work.
